# Space Intelligence Dispatch

Globally, a moderate M1.1 solar flare has nudged geomagnetic conditions to unsettled (Kp 2.0) while a Falcon 9 prepares for launch in four hours to add another 22 Starlink satellites to the constellation—a routine but relentless expansion of orbital infrastructure that now numbers in the thousands. These incremental solar disturbances and launch cadence represent the new baseline of space activity: constant, measurable, and tracked in real time across every nation's strategic interest. For stargazers, tonight offers a rare gift—a new moon, crystal-clear skies at just 13% cloud cover, and an astrophotography score of 8.0/10—conditions that won't repeat for weeks. Keep an eye on the Perseids beginning in 29 days; this quiet period is the calm before August's meteoric intensity. # Space Intelligence Dispatch

Nationally, space activity is running at a moderate clip with a Space Activity Index of 30/100 driven by a recent C3.9 solar flare and an unsettled geomagnetic storm (Kp 2.0), while SpaceX successfully launched another Starlink batch and landed its booster on the drone ship—a steady day of orbital operations across the country. For ground observers, the combination of a new moon, exceptionally clear skies (just 24% cloud cover), and an astrophotography score of 7.7/10 means tonight ranks among the best of the month for deep-sky work, even though aurora chances remain negligible in most regions.
